CURRENT AFFAIRS,

The Nationalist Lord Mayor of Dublin, Mr. Nannetti,
gave a ball which is said to have cost him—a Labour
representative—about ono thousand pounds sterling in
honour of the British Institute of Journalists and its
Trish tail. The last item of the music at the ball was
““ A Nation Once Again.”” Well, it will be very much
“once again’’ in the dim and distant future so long
as Irish journalists—including the editor and several
other prominent members of the F'reeman staff—are con-
tent to play the part of tail to the British Institute of
Journalists.

The annual dinner of this British Institute was held

- on Wednesday evening, September 6th. On the imme-

diate right of the President was the “ Saved ’’ Bishop

of Ossory; away down five places removed on the left

was the “ Idolatrous *’ Bishop of Canca. No, 2 on the

right of the President was the Bigot Provost of the Uni-
versity of spelling fame,

Cateh Tony Traill at a function in conncction with
the Oireachtas, though that function is merely Trish, and
s “non-sectarian and non-political.’”  Picturo the

Robber University giving up its examination Hall for
the holding of tho annual meeting of the dpo feip!
The first toasts were thoso of .the King, Queen, the
Prince and Princess of Wales, and the other members
of the British Royal Family, and amongst those present
were—Mr. Brayden, Editor of the Freeman; Mr. R.
Donovan, we presume also of the Simply Deplorable, and
My, J. G. MacSweeney, a F'reeman stalwart, we believe.
This “ God-Save-the-King ’’ dinner, which was attended
by the Editor of the Freeman, “ recalls,” as the “ By the
Way ’”* man might say, what is known as the “Sligo
Incident.”” It will be remembered that at a dinner of
“ National ”” teachers several declined to honour the
party toast of “ The King,”” and there was a great hub-
bub about it. The Freeman, some of whose staff fre-
quently attend “ God-Save-the-King *’ dinners, declared
at the time :— The toast of the King’s health has been
mado a party test, and ‘ God Save the King’ is a party
tune in Ireland. They are both employed as an offen-
sive challenge to Nationalists to renounce their Nationa-
lity.”” And yet Mr. Brayden sat quiet at the British
Institute of Journalists’ dinner, whilst what his paper
editorially styled this “ offensive challenge to Nationa-
lists to renounce their Nationality’’ was delivered!

Were Mr. Brayden, Mr. Donovan, and Mr. J. G.
MacSweneey—the classical author of the “ Pat o’ Nine
Tails ”’ seemingly was not present—afraid of Tony
Traill? Or perhaps the presence of Lieut.-Col. Plews,
of the Great Northern Bigot Railway, intimidated them !
And Mr. C. Hyland, of the Gaiety, where “ The Spring
Chicken ”’ is to be played, was also present. Oh, what
a press! Commenting in our issue of June 10th, 1905,
on the Freeman and the “ Sligo Incident,”” we wrote:—
“ We trust that Messrs. Brayden, M. Bodkin and Dono-
van will take up this ¢ offensive challenge’ at the first
opportunity.”’

The profits of the “Empire’’ Music Hall are not
expanding imperially. The company that runs this
place of vulgar and imported entertainment did not pay
any dividend to its shareholders this year. The profits
of the yoar were not sufficient to meet the interest on
Debenture Stock and Income Tax, for after these things
had been paid out of the profits and the £953 11s. 3d.
brought forward, there was only a diminished £610 16s.
to carry forward to next account. Mr. A. Findlater,
who is chairman of this company, stated that he had
received a letter asking why the directors did not prose-
cute Figgis, who, it will be remembered, bolted, leaving
a considerable discrepancy behind him. Mr. A. Find-
later trusted that the matter would now be let drop.
If the man Figgis was an “ Idolator ** he would be pro-
secuted quickly enough, but being one of the “ Saved "
he bolts with impunity.

One of the directors informed the shareholders that
the profits from Bung—that is, from the bar, where the
Johnnies and the Tommies like to swagger—were con-
siderable, in fact but for them they would not have a
roof over their heads. Evidently the late Dan Lowry’s
that is now the “Empire’’ has fallen on evil days.
Probably a number of the young men who, but for the
Irigh-Ireland movement, might be doing the Johnnie
and tho “ Hell of a Fellow ’’ at this place of vulgar and
imported amusement find better work with which to
ocoupy their evenings.

The Irish Collego at Ring had a successful season.
Over fifty students, including a number of priests,
attended during tho months of July and August. School
toachers from Waterford, Nenagh, Thurles, Clonmel,
Kilkenny, Birr, Roscommon, Donegal, Limorick and
Dublin were also included amongst the pupils. All the
teaching was done solely through the medium of Ivish;

and a large number of the local Irish-speaking boys and
gixls were taught to read and write Irish. There was a

alance on the right side when all expenses were paid,
and this satisfactory state of affairs was duo to the
liberal subscriptions that were received. IFor instance,
the Bishop of Waterford, Most Rev. Dr. Sheehan, con-
tributed £3 to the College; Chief O’Neill, of Chicago,
£4, and several priests gave £1 each.

The British Institute of Journalists’ crowd were in-
vited: out to Pembroke Park to ITumphrey’s Dump. Wo
read in the “ Zrish’’ Times that—" The hall in which
luncheon was served was aglow with colour, the walls
being decroated ‘with trophies of miniature Union
Jacks, while depending from a cord stretching along tho
roof were the flags of different nations.”” Mr William
Murphy, who runs the Zadependent that serves up a
half column of Irish by way of sop to Irish Irclanders
every day, proposed “ The King.""  He oxpressed the
hope that his Majesty would visit the Imposition next
year. The toast of which the Freeman said—* The
toast of the King’s health has been made a party test,
and ‘ God Save the King’ is a party tune in Ireland.
They are both employed as an offensive challenge to
Nationalists to renounce their Nationality,” was duly
honoured, the company singing the National Anthem.
Put a poor, “tame’ Catholic on horseback and you
know what to expect. We suppose William Murphy
thinks he can do everything with his money bags. Well,
William O’Brien is a more brilliant man than William
Murphy, and yet the money bags of the former don’t
appear to carry him a very long long way

Mr. Joseph Mooney, a director of the Independent,
made the statement that “now those who had opposed
and critised the project (the Imposition) were silent, or
were with it.”’ To say the least of it, this statement is
not strictly accurate. We have opposed the project, and
we are not either silent upon it or in favour of it, and
we would suggest that Mr. Joseph Mooney, who pub-
licly stated at Humphrey’s Dump that he was a tee-
totaller, should take the pledge against making wild
statements that cannot stand the test of examination.
By ths way, we would like to sec a list of the paid
officers of this Imposition. How many are belonging to
the dividend-paying religion, and how many are ““ Idola-
tors?’  Perhaps Mr. Joseph Mooney would let us know.
Mr. Shanks, a paid official of Humphrey’s Dump, said
that—*“ Tt might be worth while, however, to state that
the Exhibition was an honest and well-meant effort to
stimulate industrial and commercial progress in Ireland,
and to help the industrial revival.”” Perhaps it may be
worth while stating our conviction that it is nothing of
the kind. Why, the very imported contractors of this
glaring Tmposition were drummed out of the Dublin
Industrial Association.

Perhaps Mr. Joseph Mooney, of the Independent, was
specially digging at the F'reeman when he spoke with
great exaggeration of the opposition to the Imposition
being “silent.””  What is the F'reeman doing in the
matter? It appears now that the Freeman was not

. invited to the recent advertising luncheon held some time

ago at the ITumphrey’s Dump. The Freeman was in-
vited to the journalists’ affair last week; and we see
that nature has still left some power of natural resent-
ment, in that “truly national’’ organ. The chief re-
porters of the F'reeman and Pink wrote a letter declining
to attend the “ God-Save-the-King ’ and Union Jacky
affair at Humphrey’s Dump last week, not on national
or Irish industrial grounds, but because they werc in a
huff at being deliberately passed over on the previous
occasions!* There’s a measure of the spirit of this
“truly national”’ paper. It is against Humphrey’s
Dump. Then why, unless it is afraid of William
Murphy and his friends, does it not fight it? It is
afraid, we suppose, and even one so humble as Mr.
Joseph Mooney can publicly taunt it with being silent!

Mr. O’Callaghan, of Limerick, read a very suggestive
paper on the leather industry of Ireland at the Industrial
Conference at the Munster-Connacht Exhibition, Ho
said we imported nearly two million pounds’ worth of

boots and shoos besides large quantitics of ready-made
uppers and unwrought leathor. Yot forty years ago—
pity there was not a LEADER in existence then—the boots
worn were largely—in fact, almost altogether—hand-
made locally. Mr. O’Callaghan calculated that Ireland
spent £1,300,000 on imported leather alono that she
might casily make ab home: * Given the energy and
skill,”” said he, “ there was no l‘un'da»menbul reason why.

lughan’s paper leaves one convinced that there are great
pf.ssihi]itics of expansion in the leather trade in Ireland ;
but expansion of industry depends largely on skill and
enterprise and energy, and of these things we are rather
lacking.

“The first point of attack,” said Mr. O’Callaghan,
“on tho part of those who wished to increass their pro-

ductiveness should be the £1,800,000 worth of boots |

which were brought into the country annually.””  But
where is the enterprise to come from? In recent years

Mr. Michael Governey has started a boot factory in

Carlow, and according to Mr. O’Callaghan  all the boot
factories he knew of in Ireland seemed to be doing well.””
Yet in face of the possibilities of the industrial situation
it is taking a long time to float the contemplated boot
factory on American lines in Dublin.

Mr. O’Callaghan’s paper on the leather trade was
interesting just for the same reason that Mr. O’Shaugh-
nessy’s paper on the woollen industry was interesting.
Both were practical men who knew what they were talk-
ing about. The Irish woollen industry has progressed
rapidly. Mr. O’Shaughnessy remarked that:—

The results had demonstrated that there was good ground for
the 1t jealousy of King William’s petitioners, for it was a
gratifying fact that the best of the Irish woollen L 2S5 Was now
in the hands of the mills mentioned, and the tch and the
English manufacturers had been almost completely routed.  This
result had been achieved after a severe struggle, and was due solely
to the fact that the most up-to-date machinery was availed of, and
that no efforts wers spared to obtain the most highly trained
workmen that could be found in any country.

He gave a well-deserved rap to the woollen Dark
Brothers who have profited so largely on the LEADER’S
work in favour of Irish industry:—

Irish manufacturers, however, were inclined to depend a little
too much on their reputation, and they neglected the advertising,
which was so necessary nowadays. The result was that the man
in the street was at the mercy of the tailor, and if imported stuff
was palmed off as Irish the blame lay greatly at the door of the
Irish manufacturer who did not keep fully informed as to the trade
mark, cte. It was a strange example of the Irish manufacturer’s
horror of advertising to find big mills unrepresented at that
splendid Munster-Connacht Exhibition,

It will bo remembered that in a recent issuc we
referred to the number of Scotch importations in the
mills of Messrs. Morrogh Bros. Mr. O’Shaughuessy’s
general remarks on the importation of Englishmen and
Scotchmen are particularly interesting:—

A 1!00(.1 deal of criticism was frequently heard concerning the
importation of Englishmen and Scotchmen.  Now, it was the
obvious duty of any manufacturer who desired to be in the front
rank (o cmploy the best men that could be procured.* Ireland,
unforlunalely, had been so handicapped that 1o opportunitios were
u,hlhuml for the training up of skilled men, such as designors, dyers,
spinners, and until there was a radical change there, the best paid
posts m\:s‘l. remain largely in the hands of Englishmen and Scotch-
men.  Thero was ho\yovur @ great deal too much importation
going on, and it was timo some serious offort was made to got at
tho bottom of the avil and find a romedy. A g leal might be
dono by the manufucturcrs themselves. ~More apprentices could be
trained up in the dopartments of trades that did not require a great

wore trained in some mills,  Why not in all? And, most im-
portant of all, when they were trained, why were not young Irish-

manl I?md as well as the imported men who wero doing the same
work .

M. O’Shaughnessy referred in vigorous terms to the
Depaitment’s omission to do anything by way of dealing
with Daddy Swindle Fingers, who palms off foreign
goods as Irish. Mr. T. P. Gill subsequently stated that
they had no powers. Well, if they have not, they ought
to have them, and ought long ago to have insisted on
getting them. It is only in Treland that a great Govern-
ment Department could turn its guns on the poor insect
known as “ Daddy Long Legs,’’ and yet not have power
to lift its thumb at “ Daddy Swindle Fingers’’ behind
the counters of the country.

The weakness of these conferences such as the one
cencluded at the Munster-Connacht Exhibition is, that
thoy are only “alive’” whilst the conference lasts, and
as various important and complicated subjects are dealt
with in papers at them, the usual result is that only
brief and scrappy reports appear in the general Press,
and if full reports appear in a daily paper, a daily
paper being only “alive ”’ during the day of issue, the
chances are that they are skipped by the readers. Might
we suggest to both Mr. O’Shaughnessy, of Dripsey, and
to Mr. O’Callaghan, that they should publish their
papers in penny pamphlet form. They are important
papers, and well worthy of being widely read

By the way, “ A Manufacturer ”’ who writes to the

Press makes a neat point against Mr. Gill’s argument—
that it was not the duty of a public Department to seek
a market for commercial products. ‘“A Manufacturer ’’
asks:—“ Was it not the ‘ Department ’ that organised
the TIrish scction in St. Louis Exposition, which cost
hundreds of thousands of pounds (and no results), and
wasn’t it the Department’s staff that were on the
spot for six months secking in St. Louis for ‘a market
for commercial products?” *’ And might we add, what
is largely the duty of the vaiious forcign consuls? We
suppose the Consul service of a country may be fairly
called a “ public Department,” and the service is very
much interested in sceking markets for commercial pro-
ducts. The French Consul in Dublin, as the public
know, has been active recently with a view to stimulat-
ing trade hetwecen France and Ireland. But perhaps
they do these things better in France than in Merrion
Street.

We referred in a recent issue to the removal on promo-
tion of a bank clerk from Longford to the “Old House in
College Green,”” and to the impending nuisanco to the
locality, in the shape of a testimonial.  The incvitable
circular is before us now. It is printed on paper water-
marked “ Keltic Commereial,”’ which, subject to correc-
tion, we put down as foreign made paper. The touting
circwlar has four signatories—tho first is Matthew
McCormack, who is a “saved’’ auctioneer; the second
is Michael Gilchriest; an ¢ Idolatrous’” undertaker; tho
third is James P. Farrell, member of the Irish Parlia-
mentary Party, and, perhaps, therefore, in some people’s
estimation, though not in ours, a man who can do no
wrong; the fourth is Daniel Garraban, of whom we

know nothing.

The bank clork who is being ahiftedvirom Longford to
the “Old Flouse in College Green’” is by name J. Phillips

someothing of great and practical interest to say :—

Ill]‘ishmuu were severely handicapped by the want of an Industrial
loge, where mon could bo trained for all the positions in tho

Rogers. The touting circular runs as follows:—“ Long-
ford, September 1st, 1906. Dear Sir (or Madam),—The
romoval on promotion of Mr. J. Phillips Rogers to
Dublin is, in our opinion, an opportune time for his
friends in Longford and District to show, by some small
token of regard, their appreciation of his courtesy and
kindness whilst hore, and their satisfaction at his pro-
motion to a higher position.  Should you share this
view we will ask you to send to any of the undersigned,
on or before 15th September, a Subscription towards
making Mr. Rogers a farewell presentation, on his de-
parturc from Longford. The holiday season prevented
us convening a formal meeting to decide on what form
this should take, but if you favour us with your views
we will try to meet them. Hoping to reccive the favour
of an carly reply, we have the honour to be your obedient
servants.”’

Our view is that a bank clerk or anybody elsd is not
to be thanked for “courtesy and kindness '’ ; he should
be condemned if he did not display these ordinary com-
mon qualities. If a bank clerk was not courteous and
kind he ought to get his walking papers.  In the name
of common sense, why should the people of Longford
tax themselves—are we not all overtaxed already—be.
cause a clerk in the so-called Bank of Ireland gets a
“rise”’ from Longford to the “ Old House in College
Green?’ Notwithstanding the fact that a member of
the Irish Parliamentary Party, Mr. James P. Farrell,
is one of those attempting to raise the wind for the
young clerk in the rather exclusive  Bank of Treland,”
we hope that the people of Longford will act sensibly,
and keep their money in their pockets.

The North Leitrim Agricultural and Industrial Show
was held on the 5th inst at Dromahair.  There is a
poster of it before us. The poster is mainly an adver-
tisement of a British poultry meal. On the same day,
and we presume at the same place, there was held the
“ North Leitrim Horse, Cattle, Poultry, Home Axts,
ete., Show,”” and a poster of that, which is before us, is
mainly an advertisement of a make of British dog bis-
cuits and poultry food. ~We find by the latter poster
that the Show has received subsidies from County Com-
mittee, Congested Districts Board, and Royal Irish
Industries Association. Surely a Show that has re-
ceived subsidies from these bodies ought to get its own
posters printed without using the advertisement posters
of a British firm, and tacking on a few local announce-
ments to them. To say the least of it, it looks decidedly
mean.

Tha Hibernian Loan and Discount Bank was located
at 34 South Frederick Street—we believe this lending
corporation has now shifted to some address in Rath-
mines.  They ought to be making plenty of money
anyway. They gave a loan of £3 10s. in October last
in exchange for a bill for £5, payable in monthly in-
stalments of £1 each. What rate of interest is that?
If the customer had the use of £3 10s. for the five
months, and paid £1 10s. for the accommodation, it
would be something like one hundred per cent. per
annum; but the customer had the use of £3 10s. only
for one month ; for the sccond month, having paid off £1,
the customer had only the use of £2 10s.; during the
third month £1 10s. ; during the fourth 10s. ; and during
the fifth the use of none of it at all. Perhaps some of
our readers accustomed to these caluculations could tell
us the exact rate of interest which this customer of the
Hibernian Loan and Discount Bank paid for the accom-
modation received. It seems to us, who are not experts
in those sort of calculations, that the transaction is
equivalent to a loan of £8 for one month, for which
£1 10s. is paid by way of interest—that is at the xate of
£18 for the year, or 225 per cent. per annum.

The question of tolerance and the G.A.A. is agitating
tho athletic mind, if we may use the phrase, just now.
We sco by a local newspaper that the Dunbrody Abbey
Annual Cycling and Athletic Sparts, under G.A.A.
Laws, was held on August 26th, and that amongst j;hose
on tho Committeo was Major G. Barrett-Hamilton.
Evidently the Dunbrody people do not stand by the
rule excluding British soldiers. Or is it allowable that

a British soldier may be on a committee but may not
compete on the field?

There was rather a curious kind of week’s Irish entor-
tainment at Chapelizod recently. - It was a fancy fair,
etc., in aid of the laudable effort to clear off the debt on
the new schools.  The dancing on the first Sunday night
and during the week was, we hear, exclusively foreign.
On Sunday, the 25th August, the closing night of tho
“ Fair,”” some of the Irish Irelanders of the District wero
determined to have Irish dancing. Thoy paid and
entered the hall, but they were told that they would
have to wait half an hour until the floor was empty.
They waited a short time, and left, asking for the money
that they had paid for admission. This was refused.
The tickets which were issued for admission to the danc-
ing room were stamped on ono side ¢ Admit to Ball
Room *’ and on the other side Connpad na Faeveal,
Cpaod Seipeat loparo. In the dancing room on the Sun-
day night Tommy Atkins’s, in khaki, were there.
OQutside, in the ground, Tommy, in khaki, lent “ distinc-
‘tion ’’ to the scene. There was a roulette table; and
at the Cafe Chantant one of the songs was “Go away, Mr.
Crocodile, ‘don’t wag your tail at me,’”’ and another was
“The West’s Asleep.”” Evidently, like the youngsters
in the sweet shop, they liked a “ penn’orth of all sorts’’
at the Cafe Chantant. The members of the local branch
of the Gaelic League were to have danced a reel at the
hourly concert, and they waited in the Hall for some
time during the first Sunday night, but were not asked
to go on.  Perhaps the dignity of Mr. Crocodile would
have been offended at the sight of an Irish reel, and he
might have wagged his tail at the promoters of the
entertainment.  Penny packets of British cigarettes
were retailed by young ladies in the grounds.  Perhaps
Mr. Crocodile would have wagged his nose at Staince
cigarettes, made a fow miles away from the scene of
the entertainment by Irish labour. The posters an-
nouncing this function were headed “ Speann dzup
Séipe.” It would seem that the “Taipe” of any Gaelic
Leaguer must have been somewhat of the nature of Mr.
Crocodile’s laughter.

article in its Monday’s issue on the election of General

One of the most recent penny publications of the
Catholic Truth Society of Ireland is a booklet of 48
pages, by ‘the Rev. P. Coffey, D.Ph., S.T.L., of May-
nooth, entitled “ The Church and the Working Classes.”’
It is an opportune publication. There are a good many
cheap cchoes of what, for want of a better term, we will
call British Socialistic Raimeis ringing in Ireland just
now. The British Socialistic Raimers papers have  a
sale over here, and, in addition, as our readers know,
many of our trades unions are hitched on to those whose
headquarters are in England. We hope that Rev. Dr.
Coffey’s work is only one of many popular penny
booklets that he and others of the clergy will publish
concerning the Church and Labour. The rural working
man, the rack-rented and plundered tenant-farmer Yad
the active support of the clergy in his struggle for eman-
cipation from the slavery of unscrupulous and unchris-.
tian landlordism.  The issues that arise concerning
master and man in town employments are not so simple,
but it would be well, we think, if the clergy interested
thems:lves actively in the various labour problems that
the artisan has to face. Otherwise will he face them
himself? We doubt it. e will, to a great extent,
anyway, let British Labouwr men do the thinking and
the wire-pulling for him. The Land League, the
National League, and the United Irish League, all of
which may be regarded as rural workingmen’s trade
organisations, were, aud-are, made up of priests and
people. Are there many, priests connected with the Irish
urban workingmen’s trade organisations? Of course,
wo know that the cases are not at all parallel ; but the
perusal of Rev. Dr. Coffey’s book has suggested the
matter to our mind. If the Irish Catholic workingman
could be counted upon to| face his problems from his
Trish Catholic point of view, it might be all right; but
is he doing it? Heis, we fear, allowing British Labour
men to do a good deal of the thinking for him.

7T IIE National Assurance Company of Ircland has

sent oub by their sceretary, a man called, or who
calls himself, C. Chevallier Cream, a modest demand
for almost a quarter of a million pml'nds sLcrlmg!_ A
quarter of a million for what? To simply Lhr_ow into
the muddle-hcap that the directors of that ill-fated
company have made of a business that, \vlt]} ordinary
prudent management, shoulzl_ have been one of the most
prosperous business corporations in Ireland. ![ow the
country would gasp if an Trish company with good
prospects asked for a quarter of a million pounds ster-
ling, fully paid up as ca vital, to run some new enter-
prise. The Irish public would probably laugh at the
proposal, and say that money was not so casily plck_ed
up in Ireland. We could probably start a daily
LEADER that would revolutionise the country for-its
own good with a capita of less than £50,000; _the
Gaelic League is run, so far as its centralised operations
go, on four or five thousand a year. The United Irish
League and the Irish Parliamentary Party cost the
country—we exclude American and Australian sub-
scriptions—not more probably than £10,000 a year.
Numbers of industries could be started here, there,
and everywhere on varying capitals of from £500 to
£2,000, and many industries could be expanded by the
application of similar modest amounts of new capital.
And on the 3rd of September, in the year of grace,
1906, C. Chevallier Cream sent out a letter to the un-
fortunate shareholders of this mis-directed company
demanding that each one of them should send into
the muddle-heap constructed by the highly-respectable
and, no doubt, highly-paid directors, a sum of £6 for
every share held by them in the great financial bungle.
For the directors and their friends who hold shares,
and who are called upon to send £6 per share held into
the muddle-heap, we have no sympathy whatever;
but this demand must ring like a portent of ruin in
many a home, and, human nature being what it is, the
bitter curses of many a household will at least struggle
for expression on the heads of Mr. Pilkington, of the
Great Sourface Railway; Right Hon. Jonathan Hogg,
D.L., a director of the Bank of Ireland; William
Hone, a director of the Dublin and Kingstown Rail-
way; Arthur E. Kinahan, a director of the Bung firm

the Bank of Ireland; James Talbot Power, the “tame’’
Catholic and “loyal ”’ distiller; E. R. Read, of Joseph
Watkins and Co., beer makers; - William Robertson,
a director of the Bank of Ireland. Many a widow and
orphan will feel tempted, and perhaps many will
yield to the temptation to utter deep and bitter curses
on the heads of these men and their fellow-directors
who are responsible for the awful collapse of this so-
called National Assurance Company of Ireland.

The capital of this company is £1,000,000 nominal in
40,000 shares of £25 each. All the sharves were issued,
and £2 10s. was paid up on each. Theshareholders are
therefore liablo for a further call of £22 10s. for every

under instructions from his directors, who include
Pilkington, recently re-elected at a salary on the Great
Sourface Railway, James Talbot Power, Samuel Bew-
ley, Jonathan Hogg, Arthur E. Kinahan and J. Tod-
hunter Pim, has demanded a sum of £6 per share for
the muddle-heap which the directors and manage-
ment have made of this business that should have
been a source of great profit to the shareholders, and a
great acquisition to the Irish commercial world, A
call of £6 each on 40,000 shares works out at £240,000,
only £10,000 under an even quarter of a million
of pounds sterling. And this £240,000 when gathered

The great feature of the Irish movement, and more
particularly of that great part of it which traces its
source back to this Journal, is that it is an anti-sham
movement.  Being essentially based on reality, it can-
not, work with the make-believe, and hence it is that
the movement has jarred against the great Irish
oducational sham. It was only because the subjects
of instruction were utterly uscless that an entirely ineffi-
cient method of teaching has so long heen

THE HISTORICAL ASSOCIATION.

HE Esprit des Lois rang the demolition of the
straw-stuffed effigy of History which preceding
romancers loved to garland with the name of Art. Mon-
tesquieu neatly summed the just attitude of the historian
in the phrase, “j’ai posé les principes et j'ai vu les
histoires de toutes les nations n'en étre que les suites.”
The affable baron had no idea in that direction, perhaps,
when he wetted his quill for his masterpiece; the eigh-
teenth century saw History assume its natural
place in the category of the sciences, nevertheless. Up
to, and unfortunately sometimes since, that time History
consisted of a picture of reddened sword-gleams hedging
hundred-fight slashers, judiciously set in a background
of statues, court-scandal, and fashion-plates. I do not
mean to decry war-tales. They serve their end: wine
to an uncultivated or childish patriotism. The
blunder begins when they are dished up (curried usually
with bigotry) as beneficent History. =~ No painstaking
student of the development of national organisms would
dream of asserting that Charles the Hammer’s victory
at Poitiers dammed the Saracenic torrent which menaced
Frankish Europe in the eighth century; or that Brian’s
victory at Clontarf averted a radical transmutation of
our race; or that Pultowa sank Sweden instantaneously
to the rank of an inferior power; or that Waterloo
changed France into a republic. If Alpaide had been
never granted the joys of motherhood; if
the race of Dalgas had not even existed;
if Mazeppa had been blessed with ~the pru-
dence of Fabius; if Berthier had been able to
escape from his detention in Germany ; yet would Europe
be even as she is. . Battles are but punctuation-marks,
Massoretic points in the story of the earth, inane without
a context. History treats solely of the laws according
to which national phenomena have their being: utterly
a branch of philosophy.  Recognising History to bo
such, it must also be recognised that the study of it can
never approach universality.
The cheapening first of paper, and, following, of edu-
cation has but had the effect of bulging the pockets of

gentlemen of the type of Harmsworth and
Pearson. It has spread a thin smear of
conceited, authority-mocking information on the
old-time honest ignorance, that formerly caused

no blush on the cheek of Demos. In no country has it
made the people & whit more thoughtful. Mr. Walsh’s
idea of starting a historical association, therefore, could
not effect a widespread knowledgo of Ireland’s history,
eventuating in a heartfelt love of her. Not necessarily
would it be useless, but of that anon. He insinuates
that it is casier to acquire a knowledge of history than
of Irish. No one surely can assent to such a statement.
To obtain a practical acquaintance with a language there
is not a single one of the higher faculties required : the
highest are required for a mastery of historical science.
He remarks that love, unborn of knowledge, can never
produce devotion. As well tell me you must cold-
shoulder your parent if he sports a rich nose or suffers
from imaginativeness. e seems to believe the earth is
peopled with seraphim.  Did the barefooted peasants
who captured Wattignies under the muzzles of Austrian
guns know the history of their land? Did the brave
follows who bore O’Connell in at the top of the poll at
the Clare election of ’28 know it ?

THE ERASMUS SMITH ENDOWMENT—XXXVII,

The “ Agreement ’’ With Blue Coat.

Gems of Chicanery Set In Forgery

7Y~ HE Governors of Erasmus Smith’s Schools inter-

polated his second Rule for his Schools by forg-:
ing tho words “ or shall be erected,” in order to justify
the admission of twenty free boys dwelling within two
miles of Blue Coat Hospital, into that Hospital, and the
on of twenty free boys, of the same proximity to
tho Schools, into the Ennis School, into each of the 146
English Schools, into the Brunswick Street and Iarcourt
Street Schools.

Now for the gems of chicanery. I lmvc already given
one of those gems—namely, purchasing for £300 the
right to endow Blue Coat Hospital. The Erasmus Smith
Governors have just the same right to enter into an
« Agreement ”’ with the Board of Lord Kitchener’s
Sehool at Khartoum, to pay that Board £300 for allow-
ing the said Governors to support, clothe, and educate
twenty Dervishes in the said School, and afterwards
apprentice them to trades.

The second gem. The Charter has directed that the
twenty free boys should be chosen by at least seven of
the Governors, who should include the Treasurer. The
Governors got enacted, by the Act of 1723, that the Lord
Mayor and Recorder of Dublin, who were ex-officio
members of the Board of Blue Coat Hospital, and two of
tho Aldermen of the said city, such as the Governors of
the said Hospital should elect, shall be standing Gover-
nors of the Schools founded by Erasmus Smith, and
added to the thirty-two Governors appointed by the
Charter; and also that the Treasurer and three of the
Governors of the said Schools, such as the said Gover-
nors should clect, should be standing Governors, and
added to the Governors of the said Flospital. Thus on
the Board of Blue Coat Hospital there were eight
Governors of the Erasmus Smith Board, including the
Treasurer—namely, the Lord Mayor, the Recorder, and
two Aldermen of Dublin, and the Treasurer and three
other members of the Erasmus Smith Board. Thus they
had put on the Board of Blue Coat Hospital one more
than the necessary number appointed by the Charter to
nominate the twenty free boys. And this structure of
fraud was crowned by the third gem, which consisted in
paying the Schoolmaster of Blue Coat Hospital out of
the Erasmus Smith Endowment for teaching the twenty
boys. Thus the School in Blue Coat Hospital was
turned into an Erasmus Smith School, under eight
Governors of the Erasmus Smith Board, who, accordiag
to the Charter, improved by forgery, had authority to
educate and clothe twenty poor boys dwelling withi
two miles of the School.

By the same trick the Governors could enter into an
“Agtezment’’ with the Board of the South Dublin Union,
or of any other Union, to support, educate, clothe, and
apprentice, at the Governors’ expense, twenty poor boys
dwelling within two miles of the Union Workhouse.
Nothing easier. Simply make four of the members of
the Union members of the Erasmus Smith Board, and
four of the members of the Erasmus Smith Board, in-
cluding the Treasurcr, members of the Union, and pay
the Schoolmaster out of the Endowment for teaching
tho twenty boys, and the thing is done.

This whole “ Agreement,”” by which some £150,000
of the Endowment have been spent on the maintenance
and education of Dublin Protestant urchins, is founded
on a series of frauds.

1. The money has been paid out of an alleged legally-
defined surplus, which has never existed.

2. Even if there were a surplus, there was no legal
right to expend it on giving free education, clothing
and apprenticing to any boy dwelling within two miles
of Blue Coat Hospital. Such a right extends only to
peor boys dwelling within two miles of Drogheda, Gal-
way and Tipperary.

3. There was no right whatever, or semblance of
right, to expend any of the money of the Endowment on
supporting boys in Blue Coat Hospital. From the part
